metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Augment AC4's proving command (or add a companion unit test) that instantiates the vague fixture, runs it through the verifier in a controlled offline call or mock, and asserts the `remediation` field in the returned finding is non-empty — or explicitly state that AC6 (the live eval run) is the sole measurable proof and that AC4 case (b) validates structure only.
findings.finding: AC4 case (b) requires the eval spec's scorer/assertion to verify that the resulting finding's `remediation` field is 'non-empty and carries the record-proof coaching', but the stated proving command (`validate_eval_spec(strict=True) == []`) only checks spec structural validity — it cannot verify that the scorer actually asserts on the remediation field content (fixture invalidity, defect type 4).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either register a new deterministic scorer (e.g. `finding_has_remediation_coaching`) in `eval_scorers.py` that checks non-empty `remediation` on FAIL findings, or explicitly document in AC4 that the `remediation` assertion is covered only by the LLM judge (and update the case to use a judge rubric for that check rather than implying deterministic coverage).
findings.finding: AC4's `CV-operator-attested-vague` case requires the assertion 'finding's `remediation` field is non-empty and carries the record-proof coaching', but no deterministic scorer in the `REGISTRY` checks per-finding `remediation` content, and the plan does not name a new scorer to add. The `test_new_eval_specs.py` auto-coverage check (`validate_eval_spec(strict=True)`) only validates scorer names are registered — it cannot enforce this per-finding field assertion unless a new scorer is registered.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the eval spec design whether the `ticket_context` description is the intended vehicle for attestation text in eval fixtures, OR specify that `eval_solver.py` needs a `comments` fixture key that calls `rebar.comment(tid, body, repo_root=root)` after ticket creation — and if the latter, add it to the Scope IN list.
findings.finding: The eval solver (`eval_solver.py`) has no mechanism to seed ticket COMMENTS into disposable eval fixtures — it only writes `ticket_context` as the ticket description. The three new operator-attested eval cases (CV-operator-attested-concrete, CV-operator-attested-vague, CV-attestation-with-injection) require attestation evidence that the verifier reads via `show_ticket`; the plan describes this as 'a ticket_context comment naming a change id + vote outcome + timestamp,' but the solver calls `rebar.create_ticket(description=ticket_context)` and never calls `rebar.comment()` on the disposable ticket. If the implementer embeds the attestation inside the description (the only available vehicle), the test works but validates description-embedded attestations — not the comment-as-evidence path that production operators will use. If the implementer tries to seed a real comment, the solver needs extension that is out of scope and unspecified.
findings.location: AC4 (four new eval dataset cases) / `src/rebar/llm/evals/eval_solver.py` lines 193–201 / `src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/completion-verifier.eval.yaml`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `comments: list[str]` field to eval case data and extend `eval_solver.py`'s `completion-verifier` arm: after `create_ticket`, iterate `case.get('comments') or []` and call `rebar.comment(tid, body, repo_root=root)` for each. This faithfully seeds the production evidence channel and makes the fixture self-documenting. If description-embedding is intentional instead, note it explicitly in the eval spec comment and rename the AC4 description from 'a ticket_context comment' to 'ticket_context text that includes an attestation'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify concretely how the `remediation`-non-empty assertion for `CV-operator-attested-vague` is expressed in the eval spec: either register a new deterministic scorer (e.g., `operator_attested_finding_has_remediation`) in `eval_scorers.py` REGISTRY and add it to the spec, OR explicitly accept that this assertion is advisory-only (non-gating LLM judge rubric) and note the limitation in the ADR.
findings.finding: AC4 specifies that the `CV-operator-attested-vague` case must assert 'the resulting finding's `remediation` field [is] non-empty and carries the record-proof coaching,' but the eval framework's gating mechanism works only through registered deterministic scorer names (`known_scorer_names()` in `eval_scorers.py`) or non-gating LLM judges. No registered scorer checks per-finding `remediation`. If the implementer adds a new deterministic scorer name for this assertion without registering it in `REGISTRY`, `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` rejects the spec. If expressed as part of the LLM judge rubric, the assertion is advisory-only (non-gating). The plan specifies neither a new scorer name nor which of the two mechanisms carries this assertion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a deterministic scorer (e.g., `finding_remediation_non_empty`) or extend `_schema_verdict` to check that FAIL findings for operator-attested criteria carry a non-empty `remediation` field, and register it so the `CV-operator-attested-vague` case can assert on it.
findings.finding: The AC4b eval case (CV-operator-attested-vague) requires an assertion that the resulting finding's `remediation` field is non-empty, but no scorer — deterministic or LLM-judge — is defined or named in the plan to enforce this assertion. The REGISTRY in eval_scorers.py has no entry that inspects a finding-level `remediation` field, and the existing `emits_valid_verdict` scorer only checks `citations` presence (line 168-170), not `remediation`. An executor building the eval case has no specified mechanism to satisfy the assertion.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a deterministic scorer `operator_attested_finding_has_remediation` to eval_scorers.py that, for cases tagged with `criterion_kind: operator-attested` (add a case-level metadata key), inspects each FAIL finding for a non-empty `remediation` field. Register it in REGISTRY and name it in the `CV-operator-attested-vague` case's scorer list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a concrete scorer or LLM-judge rubric for the `CV-attestation-with-injection` case that distinguishes verdict-by-attestation from verdict-by-command (e.g., an `llm-judge` rubric that reads the finding's `detail`/`summary` for acknowledgment of the command and credit of the factual specific, or a `note`-driven deterministic scorer on `summary` text content).
findings.finding: The AC4c eval case (CV-attestation-with-injection) requires 'a scorer/rubric asserting the PASS was reached by crediting the factual specific while ignoring the command,' but the plan never specifies which scorer name, whether it is a new deterministic scorer or an LLM-judge, or how it is wired. No matching scorer name exists in eval_scorers.py REGISTRY. The `expect: pass` + `no_false_fail_on_complete` scorer pair would fire only on PASS/FAIL verdict — it cannot distinguish *why* a PASS was reached (factual credit vs. command obeying).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for AC5, such as a grep against the eval spec YAML for the specific rubric key/text that permits ticket-text citations: e.g. `grep -Eiq 'ticket|attestation' src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/completion-verifier.eval.yaml`.
findings.finding: AC5's verification method is 'Verified by reading the eval spec; covered by the AC5 validate_eval_spec command' — but the specific claim (that the grounding rubric accepts ticket-text citation for operator-attested findings) is a semantic/behavioral property that validate_eval_spec does not test, leaving AC5 with no machine-executable proving command.
findings.location: AC5 (grounding rubric relaxation) — 'Verified by reading the eval spec'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete mechanism for making the `finding_cites_criterion_and_is_grounded` rubric case-conditional for operator-attested findings — e.g., add a `criterion_kind: operator-attested` metadata key to the eval case schema and thread it into the rubric prompt, or add a separate `operator_attested_finding_grounded` judge scorer used only by the new cases.
findings.finding: AC5 says the existing `finding_cites_criterion_and_is_grounded` LLM-judge rubric must be 'updated (or made case-conditional) to accept a source/ticket-text citation as valid grounding for an operator-attested finding,' but the plan does not specify the mechanism by which the rubric knows which cases are operator-attested. The eval spec case format has no `criterion_kind` or equivalent metadata key, and the rubric is a global prompt string — it cannot currently branch on per-case metadata.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a second LLM-judge scorer `operator_attested_finding_grounded` with a rubric that explicitly accepts ticket-text `source` citations as valid evidence, and use it only on the operator-attested cases. Keep the existing `finding_cites_criterion_and_is_grounded` scorer unchanged for codebase-verifiable cases.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the AC cross-references in the Scope bullet for `completion-verifier.eval.yaml`: change 'AC5' → 'AC4' (four dataset cases) and 'AC7' → 'AC5' (grounding rubric relaxation).
findings.finding: The Scope bullet for `completion-verifier.eval.yaml` mis-labels AC references: it says 'the FOUR named dataset cases in AC5 below' but those cases appear in AC4, and says 'relax the existing grounding rubric (AC7)' but the grounding rubric criterion is AC5. An implementer cross-referencing Scope to find the eval cases would land on the wrong criterion.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Scope bullet to: '…add the FOUR named dataset cases in AC4 below AND relax the existing grounding rubric (AC5) so operator-attested findings may cite ticket-text.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default design decision in AC5: prefer 'case-conditional' (scoped to operator-attested cases only) so that codebase-verifiable grounding requirements are unchanged, and remove the 'or' optionality.
findings.finding: AC5 states the grounding rubric should be 'updated (or made case-conditional)' — the 'or' leaves a real design choice (global relaxation vs. conditional per case type) deferred to the executor, which could produce incompatible implementations depending on which path is chosen.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'updated (or made case-conditional)' with 'made case-conditional: the relaxed grounding (ticket-text citation is valid) applies only to findings for operator-attested criteria; codebase-verifiable findings retain the existing file/symbol citation requirement.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document in `completion_verifier.md` and `CLAUDE.md` whether tag matching is case-insensitive and exact, and specify that when a tag is present but unrecognized the verifier should note this in its finding rather than silently falling back to codebase-verifiable.
findings.finding: The `[operator-attested]` tag convention — a new user-facing authoring surface — does not specify behavior when an author writes a near-miss variant (e.g., `[operator_attested]`, `[Operator-Attested]`, `[operational]`). The plan's fallback is 'treat as codebase-verifiable (stricter)', meaning a typo silently downgrades the criterion without any coaching to the author that their tag was unrecognized. The plan covers the absent-tag case but not the malformed-tag case.
findings.suggested_fix: In the prompt, add a rule: 'If a tag is present but does not exactly match `[operator-attested]` or `[codebase]` (case-insensitive), note in the finding that the tag is unrecognized and the criterion is being evaluated under the codebase-verifiable bar.' Add the same note to the CLAUDE.md authoring guidance with the canonical exact form.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge the relevant prompt-injection prior art (e.g., indirect injection research) in the ADR and explain why the threat model (haste, not deception) bounds the risk; add at least one adversarial variant to the AC4c eval fixture (a comment that looks factual but is actually a verdict command) to provide minimal empirical grounding before committing.
findings.finding: The command-vs-attestation discrimination (injection guard split rule) is a novel capability claim — that the LLM can reliably distinguish a 'command that controls the verdict' from an 'attestation that reports a checkable fact' in the same ticket comment — but no prior art, empirical baseline, or red-team result is cited to ground this before committing to it as a design pillar.
findings.location: What section, item 3: 'Split the injection guard from factual trust'; AC2 / AC4c eval fixture CV-attestation-with-injection

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Anchor the AC3 proving command to the new field on `VerdictFinding` specifically, e.g. `grep -E 'class VerdictFinding' src/rebar/llm/contracts.py` combined with `grep -E 'remediation\s*:' src/rebar/llm/contracts.py` scoped after that class definition, or add a unit test that instantiates `VerdictFinding(remediation='x')` and asserts the field is present.
findings.finding: The AC3 proving command `grep -q 'remediation' src/rebar/llm/contracts.py` is a defect-type-1 substring false-positive: the plan explicitly states the EXISTING top-level `remediation` field is KEPT for back-compat, so that grep will pass even if the new per-finding `remediation` field on `VerdictFinding` was never added.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the grep with a Python assertion that directly tests the new field: `python -c "from rebar.llm.contracts import completion_verdict_response_model; m = completion_verdict_response_model(); f = m.__fields__['findings'].outer_type_.__args__[0]; assert 'remediation' in f.__fields__, 'VerdictFinding missing remediation field'"`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in Scope and AC7 whether the 'short section in `docs/`' is a separate file from the ADR (and if so, specify its path and add a `test -f` to AC7's proving command) or confirm it IS the ADR and remove the ambiguous phrasing.
findings.finding: The Scope section describes two separate deliverables — 'A new ADR `docs/adr/0043-…`' AND 'a short section in `docs/`' (linked from `completion_verifier.md`) — but AC7's proving command only verifies the ADR file and CLAUDE.md. If the 'short section in docs/' is a separate file from the ADR, it has no path specified, no proving command, and no AC coverage.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a Scope item (or an explicit out-of-scope acknowledgment) covering the retired-synonym audit: either update `infra/runbooks/two-vote-gate-rollback.md` and any other docs that use the retired terms, or explicitly note they are grandfathered and point readers to the ADR for the canonical names.
findings.finding: The plan retires several canonical synonyms ('out-of-codebase', 'operational', 'runtime', 'codebase', 'verify against code') but the Scope does not include auditing or updating existing documents that use those terms — specifically `infra/runbooks/two-vote-gate-rollback.md` (cited in the Why section as using 'operational' terminology) and any other docs/comments that may reference the retired labels. These become stale references once the new terminology is canonical.
findings.location: Canonical terminology section; Scope (IN list); Why section citing `infra/runbooks/two-vote-gate-rollback.md` C.1
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope IN list: '`infra/runbooks/two-vote-gate-rollback.md` — replace retired terms ("operational", "out-of-codebase") with their canonical equivalents and add a footnote referencing ADR 0043.' If a full sweep is impractical, add a single sentence to the ADR: 'Prior documents may use the retired terms; ADR 0043 is the authoritative terminology reference.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether 'a short section in `docs/`' is (a) a separate file (specify its path and add a `test -f` check to AC7's proving command) or (b) prose within the ADR itself (remove the 'short section in docs/' phrasing and update `completion_verifier.md` to link to the ADR directly).
findings.finding: The Scope section names two separate documentation artifacts — 'A new ADR `docs/adr/0043-operator-attested-completion-evidence.md` + a short section in `docs/` (linked from `completion_verifier.md`)' — but AC7's proving command only verifies the ADR file and the CLAUDE.md update. If 'a short section in `docs/`' is a distinct deliverable (not just prose inside the ADR), it has no acceptance criterion, no proving command, and no specified file path, making it un-verifiable and potentially forgotten.
findings.location: Scope IN list (ADR + short section sentence); AC7 (proving command)
findings.suggested_fix: If it is the same document: replace 'A new ADR … + a short section in docs/ (linked from completion_verifier.md)' with 'A new ADR … which completion_verifier.md links to.' If it is a separate how-to page, add it to the Scope with a concrete path (e.g., `docs/operator-attested-evidence.md`) and extend the AC7 proving command: `&& test -f docs/operator-attested-evidence.md`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/test_new_eval_specs.py` to the Scope IN list (or confirm it already exists under a different path) — it is cited in AC4 as the auto-coverage mechanism for `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` but is absent from the codebase.
findings.finding: `tests/unit/test_new_eval_specs.py` does not exist in the codebase, yet the plan cites it as the auto-coverage guarantee for `validate_eval_spec(strict=True) == []` in AC4. The plan scopes in only changes to `completion-verifier.eval.yaml`, `contracts.py`, `completion_verdict.schema.json`, the ADR, and `CLAUDE.md` — creating this test file is neither listed in Scope nor in any AC proving command, so the coverage it is supposed to provide is currently absent.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `tests/unit/test_new_eval_specs.py` to the **Scope > IN** bullet list. The file should call `validate_eval_spec(load_eval_spec('completion-verifier'), strict=True) == []` and any other new specs to prevent schema regressions.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific `docs/` file that will host the 'where does DONE evidence live' short section (e.g. `docs/completion-evidence-model.md` or a new section appended to `docs/llm-framework.md`) and add it to both the Scope and the AC7 proving command.
findings.finding: The Scope names 'a short section in `docs/` (linked from `completion_verifier.md`)' as an edit target but does not identify the specific file path. No existing `docs/` file obviously hosts this section, so implementors have no canonical target and cannot write a deterministic proving command for it.
findings.location: Scope section — '`A new ADR docs/adr/0043-... + a short section in docs/`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Soften or justify the 'deterministic from the tag' claim: either cite prior empirical evidence that inline-tag mode-switching is reliable in LLM evaluators, add a robustness note (e.g., the fallback rule double-covers misclassification), or replace 'deterministic' with 'rule-based / default-conservative' and explain why misclassification is bounded.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that LLM classification of an `[operator-attested]` tag is 'deterministic from the tag' — but LLM behavior is probabilistic, and this capability claim is unverified. No empirical baseline, ablation, or prior art is cited to support that a one-token inline tag reliably switches evaluation mode across model versions or prompt variations.
findings.location: Rationale section: "the classification is deterministic from the tag, so behavior on all existing (untagged) tickets is unchanged"
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the classification is deterministic from the tag' with 'the classification rule is tag-driven with a conservative default (unmarked → codebase bar), so any misclassification — failing to read the tag — falls back to the stricter bar rather than the permissive one.' This is both more accurate and strengthens the safety argument.

findings.finding: The 'Rationale' section says the verifier 'falls back to its own reading of the criterion when the tag is absent,' implying it may classify an untagged criterion as operator-attested based on content. But the 'AC-authoring convention' section and AC1 both say 'Fallback for an unmarked criterion: default to codebase-verifiable' and the verifier 'does NOT fail a criterion merely for lacking a tag.' These are contradictory: one tells the implementer to write the prompt so the LLM infers the kind from content; the other tells them to hardcode the fallback to codebase-verifiable regardless of content.
findings.location: 'Rationale' section ('falling back to its own reading') vs. 'AC-authoring convention' section ('Fallback for an unmarked criterion: default to codebase-verifiable') and AC1 ('the unmarked→codebase-verifiable fallback')
findings.suggested_fix: Remove 'falling back to its own reading of the criterion when the tag is absent' from the Rationale and replace it with 'falling back to codebase-verifiable (the default) when the tag is absent' to match the convention section and AC1. This preserves the stricter bar and eliminates the ambiguity for the prompt implementer.

findings.finding: AC1, AC2, and AC6 each specify 'Verified by reading the prompt file / the files' as their proving check, which is a human-review action rather than a deterministic, reproducible command that produces pass/fail evidence. An executing agent has no concrete grep or structured assertion to run, so verification could silently pass even if a required element is absent or only partially present.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1, AC2, AC6 (verification clauses)
findings.suggested_fix: For AC1/AC2 append: `(Proving command: grep -cE 'operator-attested|codebase-verifiable|verifiable specific|command.*verdict|attestation.*fact' src/rebar/llm/reviewers/completion_verifier.md — output must be ≥5 matching lines, one per required element.)` For AC6 append a parallel grep against the ADR file asserting the five named sections are present.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
